s2.explain: GTD originates from trophoblastic tissue of the placenta.

s3.q: GTD is classified into all EXCEPT:
s3.explain: GTD includes hydatidiform mole (complete & partial), invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, and placental site trophoblastic tumor.

s4.explain: Hydatidiform mole is characterized by vesicular swelling of the chorionic villi resembling grapes.

s5.q: The incidence of hydatidiform mole is highest in:
s5.explain: Highest incidence is in Southeast Asia (1:200-400 pregnancies) vs 1:1000-2000 in North America/Europe.

s6.q: A complete mole has a karyotype of:
s6.explain: Complete mole is usually 46,XX (all paternal chromosomes, empty egg fertilized by haploid sperm that duplicates). Partial mole is triploid (69,XXY) - two paternal + one maternal.

s6.q: Which type of hydatidiform mole may have fetal tissue present?
s6.explain: Partial mole may have fetal tissue present (though usually non-viable). Complete mole has no fetal tissue.

s7.q: Risk factors for hydatidiform mole include all EXCEPT:
s7.explain: Risk factors: maternal age >40, previous mole, low socioeconomic status, and vitamin A deficiency.

s8.q: The classic gross pathology description of hydatidiform mole is:
s8.explain: Grossly, the mole appears as grape-like clusters of vesicles due to dilated chorionic villi.

s10.q: A classic clinical feature of hydatidiform mole is:
options: Uterus larger than expected for dates
s10.explain: The uterus is larger than expected for dates (50% of cases) along with vaginal bleeding, hyperemesis, and absent FHS.

s10.q: Theca lutein cysts may be associated with:
s10.explain: Bilateral theca lutein ovarian cysts may occur due to high hCG levels, seen more with complete mole.

s12.q: On ultrasound, a hydatidiform mole classically appears as:
s12.explain: Ultrasound shows a snowstorm or honeycomb pattern with no fetal parts or fetal heart activity.

s13.q: In hydatidiform mole, hCG levels are:
s13.explain: hCG levels are markedly elevated above normal pregnancy levels due to excessive trophoblastic proliferation.

s14.q: The primary treatment for hydatidiform mole is:
s14.explain: Suction evacuation and curettage (S&E) is the primary treatment. Hysterectomy may be considered if no future fertility desired.

s15.q: Postmolar follow-up is essential primarily to detect:
options: Development of persistent trophoblastic disease or choriocarcinoma
s15.explain: Follow-up with weekly hCG until negative for 3 consecutive weeks, then monthly for 6 months to detect persistent disease/choriocarcinoma.

s16.q: Contraception after molar pregnancy should be maintained for:
s16.explain: Contraception for 6-12 months is recommended to avoid confusion between new pregnancy and rising hCG from persistent disease.

s17.q: Chemotherapy for GTD is indicated in all EXCEPT:
options: Rising or plateauing hCG after evacuation
s17.explain: Chemotherapy is indicated for persistent/rising hCG, metastatic disease, choriocarcinoma, and invasive mole.
